transcript.transcript: Hello, my name is Nancy Inaba, and I am, uh, part of the committee to celebrate and record the History of Idaho Watercolor Society, which turns 50 years in 2026. Today's date is September the 30th, 2025. And I am with Gloria miller Allen to talk about, um, the history of Idaho Watercolor Society, her journey as an artist, and some insights into her success as an artist. This oral history is conducted with the support of Idaho Idaho Historical Society, sorry as part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. The Oral History Project, of which this interview is a part, supports a commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognize, appreciate, and commemorate Idaho's journey into America's legacy. Gloria miller. Firstly, thank you so much for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences about the focus of the interview. Um. Through these questions, I hope to gain insight into, um, the Journey of Idaho Watercolor Society and your journey as an artist. I will begin the interview with some general questions, and we can get into more specific topics from there. During the interview, I may adjust the order of questions to allow for some natural flow. So before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record this interview? And can I have you state your name and spell your first and last name? Yes, you have my permission. And my name is Gloria g l o r i a M I l l e r A l len. Okay. Thank you. All right, let me add my maiden name in there, because when I got married, he said, well, I guess you won't be signing your name Gloria Miller anymore. And I thought, yes, I will. So I have. There's your independent streak. You're a woman before your time. I know I have read a lot about you in your bio, and we may upload that as part of the book. Can you tell me something about yourself? I know you've reflected on that in the past. Well, um, I remember when I was a very small child, they were building Disneyland. And I remember thinking I wanted to be a Disney artist. I watched those programs when they were building it, and I thought, that is like what was like my dream when I was a little kid. I really wanted to be an artist, but I had artists on some kind of a pedestal. It was silly, but I had that pedestal anyway. Um, artists are people that work hard. That's what they are. But. And so there's no pedestal. But I didn't know that, of course. And I just, uh. But I didn't get to be a Walt Disney artist, but, um. But I wanted to be an artist my whole entire life, And and I and I got to be, which I am very grateful for. So, um, as far as the rest of me is concerned, I grew up in the West, in Kansas, um, and Missouri. My dad was a pilot and we moved around a lot. Um, my sister is also an artist, and my brother was a jazz musician. So my all, uh, arty, artsy people, um, and then, uh, when I got married, I really I still painted a little bit, but not as much because I really got very, very involved with. And, um, I have three children that I birthed and raised. And so as they got older, my art got bigger and bigger. And pretty soon it wasn't a hobby anymore. It was. Me. Actually. Just me. So anyway, that's that's kind of me in a nutshell. Okay. Alright. And what brought you to Idaho? Um, when my husband and I were married, he was in college, and he got his PhD in chemistry, physics and math and at Oregon State University. And we first moved to new Jersey, where we lived for five years when our children were small. And then he got an opportunity to come to Idaho National Laboratory. And we moved here in Idaho in 1970 and basically our kids. But it was the national lab that brought us here, and I didn't know at the time. But Idaho Falls, which is where I live, had a wonderful art community, and it has grown and grown and grown since I've been here. And so we came here because of science and found out that it was perfect for both of us. The wonderful art community and wonderful art friends and the Idaho Watercolor Society was certainly a big part of how I got to be a charter member. So, um, remember when the announcement came that there was going to be a new watercolor group starting up, and was I interested? And yes, I was so, so I joined up and it's been a real blessing. Well, Idaho Falls. That is a unique community in terms of the support for the arts. Uh, it's unique compared to every place else here in Idaho. It is. Uh, and I will say that arts in Idaho, I think it was Eagle Rock Art Guild was the very first art guild in Idaho. And it has been going all these years. It's like every volunteer organization just had ups and downs and ups and downs, but it's hung in there and it's has spawned an enormous, um, other things: Pencils Society, we have,the Art Museum of eastern Idaho, which I was very involved with. Um, and, um, we have, Plein aire painters. We have just an enormous, diverse group of people and groups that people can join. Almost always find a group here and Idaho Falls, and. Local area, there's any number. So it is very active. And tremendous community support for the arts, which is also very unique. Let's see. Well, You have been an artist for a long time. And as I looked at your your bibliography or your bio, I was impressed with how much you have accomplished plus raising children. How did you prioritize is your time to accomplish so much. Quite frankly, when my children were young, they were first. I didn't do a lot of work. Um, I, I did some always I because I can't hardly help it, but I got very involved with scouts and all kinds of kid activities and my, my scouts, from Cub Scouts to brownies to all the Girl Scouts and Boy Scout. Things always involved a lot of art stuff. We designed our scenery. We made puppets. It was always kind of artsy, so I was even, you know, I was kind of doing it then, too. But really, when my children were younger, I really wasn't a full time artist by any means. It was still in the back of my mind. I really wanted to be, but I just, um, all invested. Mhm. The number of years, and the older they got, the more I got into the arts. Mhm. Yeah. You know um there's, you can't serve too many priorities otherwise it defies the word priority. And I have no regrets about that. Um, it truly was a decision I made and I wanted to do it that way. And then as I got so that I had a little bit more time that I could do what I wanted, I. I just did it bigger and bigger as my kids got bigger. Mhm. Okay. So I know that you've been very successful as an artist, but you've also held roles within IWS within Idaho Watercolor Society. And what's unique to our society is the regional directors. Can you speak something to that. I can um, I was, I believe, the second, um, regional rep in Idaho. I believe Deanne Hally is no longer with us was the first one. I think she did that for a few years. And then I took over I think I think I was going to say 12 years, maybe I did that, I'm not sure. Followed by Ginger Kramer for a similar time. followed by Linda Carlson, and now Les Scott. Our region has been very lucky. But during that time I hosted a lot of nationally known watercolorists that came to teach in Idaho for the Idaho Watercolor Society. And in those days we had in the earlier days, we had workshops in Boise and Idaho Falls and sometimes there in northern Idaho or Pocatello. But I think it was I think there was one in northern Idaho. I think they stayed for three weeks and did three workshops, and that is no longer the case. But that time it was. And so I got to take advantage of that. I got to pick them up at the airport. I got to be their hostess, you know, took them out to dinner or whatever. But I just learned so much. I can't even begin to say how much I learned because of that. So, you know, you volunteer for things like us. You're giving. But you know what? You're getting a lot, too. And I was really just delighted to have that opportunity, and I can't I didn't get to go to college to study art, but I got to go to IWS. And . Yeah. And, um, and I taught and I, I not only learned about a lot about painting, but I learned a lot about teaching, you know, from them, because some of them were such great teachers and shared so much. And I, um, I have really enjoyed teaching. I have no idea when I first started all this that I would ever be able to teach around the country, but what happened is when you get AWS and NWS Signature membership, get asked to teach. And and I didn't even know that was going to happen, frankly. And then I started shaking in my boots about that, um, because I put together my workshop and spent a lot of time planning that. And it's been another joy, really went around teaching, I found out I love teaching. Um. I got to do that too. Right? I love the introduction you have on your website about what you'll gain from taking this class. Um, describing more about the creativity to finding yourself. Oh, about the the page on my workshops. Yes, yes. Yeah, I my workshop is not just watercolors or techniques. It's basically. And um and so I. Since I have wanted to do this my whole life, I know there's lots of other people just like me that want to do that, and it's just a really a joy to them. Do it. And I can't really put words to really how much of a joy it is because it is very rewarding. I love my students. Yeah. Um, I wish I. Well, I'm going to be on the lookout for your workshops. I just finished one at the art museum. Yeah, I saw that, I saw that I missed that one. Um, I just finished reading your book, and I was not aware that you were such a beautiful poet. Your ability to reflect your inner experience as an artist. And what intrigues you? Well, I have thousands of poems. Some of them are about art and the creative process. But I really believe my heart that artists and and I make a broad sweep with that term, because I know that there's art can play a lot of parts, everything from a hobby to a profession. But whatever little, little or huge part it plays all of those. All of us have a kind of a built in. Natural sense of design. And I don't think people realize that they even have that. And my what I love to do is have them realize that they do. Yes. And develop that and to. To reach down and pull it up because it is there. And I think the main thing is, is discovering that it is there and realizing and accepting that gift. Yes, human beings are very creative. Artists get a lot of credit for being creative, but scientists are creative too, right? I mean, it goes on and on. Creative creation. Humans are creative creatures. And so to accept that gift of creativity is a big step in naming yourself as an artist. Claiming that you are. Because I often think creativity is overlooked when you read about artists in the artist magazine. It's more about technique that they speak of, but it's almost like perhaps it's assumed. But maybe it also has to do with permission. It has a great deal, a great deal to do with that. I was probably in my late 30s or early 40s when I gave myself permission to say, I am an artist. I always was, but I told you about that pedestal that I had artists on. Uh, so I, I, I think I always have. I know, I always have. Lions were awesome, but, um, I, I truly think that giving yourself permission to be an artist and as occupation as an artist, I think that is a very big step for some of us. I think other people can do that much more easily, but I had a hard time doing that. Um, but when I finally said, I am an artist, that's it. I'm an artist. It was a big, big step for me. And it changes the way you look at everything. Um. It changes the way you look at what you're doing. Um. And it it's a big step. You're right. It is a very big problem. Mhm. It's, um. Uh, yeah. I feel like we could talk about this forever. I think something you've given some thought to. Um. So when you reflect back over your long history with IWS, you know, we've had ups and downs and membership and enthusiasms and projects that have been successful and others not. Um, is there anything that you can identify that seems to really work for IWS to keep it a vibrant organization or things that don't work well? I was very active for many years. I have not been very active the last 15 years or more. Um, because I just, I don't know, I just got I got carried away. But but I believe that the thing that IWS was very successful at is bringing these people in to teach. And I feel like having our juried show is a really big deal, because I think when you have a goal to get into a show, that it's always good to have a goal and something to work for. Um, and so I think that the show and the jurors brought in have been really huge. And I think nowadays it's getting to be with YouTube and all this stuff that's available. I don't know what the future lies for workshop teachers. Um, I'm, I'm. I really don't know. It is changing. It is evolving and it's changing. I don't know where it will go from here, but that to me has been. And then Also not to underestimate the friendships that are formed and the art friends are very important and artists because most of the time when you're working as an artist, you're working alone. And that's fine for me. I don't mind that at all, but I do love my art friends and that I've met that are other artists around wherever I've gone. Um, I think art or art wonderful to be with because they get you, you know, you get each other. You just don't have to explain what's going on because you you get that we have our own thing. I think, you know, golfers like to hang with golfers, and painters like to hang with painters. It's an important thing and IWS and and has done that and I hope it can continue. Yes, I hope so too. Well, do you have any, um, like, memories that were particularly outstanding in your time with IWS? It sounds like your time with artists when you were, uh, hosting the the jurors and the teachers, but any other idea or memories come to mind? Well, um, some funny ones. Um, I had, uh. Well, let me start with Ruth Clark, because I did know her. She was one of the founders of the Idaho Watercolor Society, and she just had, I think, when she moved to Idaho, she said, where is the Idaho Watercolor Society? And somebody said, there is none. And she said, why not? And that's kind of, you know, how it all I'm sure that's very simple, but you get the idea. I think it was just her idea to. Maybe we should have one. Mhm. And that Idaho, um, is in the arts I think is a little bit of a surprise to the rest of the country because it, because I mean I think people in New York aren't sure we have flush toilets. But we do. And we also have a, we have like I said, we have a good rich bunch of artists here in our state, for sure. We do. We have much to be proud of. Do we have a lot. We have many, many fine watercolors in particular. I know the most, of course, was Ruth Clark. She was elderly when I first met her. Uh, but she used to come to my booth when I used to do the Boise Art in the park. Mhm. Right. That for many, many years, um. And she would come and sit in my booth. And so I got to know her that way. And that was a real joy. And so having her I think it was just great. Uh, and then really, one of the other funny things about our founders, one of our other founders was Julie Rifle. Okay. I've heard of her. I've heard her name. Joyce has mentioned her name. And I have a funny story about her. I didn't really get to know Julie really well because she lived in Boise. I lived over in Idaho Falls, but we I would go to annual meetings and stuff. I was regional rep and I kind of got to know her a little bit. One time at Julia Davis Park, it was my birthday in September. And so one morning before the event started, everyone setting up their booth and everything, and Julie came waltzing across the green where people were setting up their booths in a belly dancing outfit, carrying a carrying a lantern and a boombox, which was playing belly dancing music. And she comes dancing across with a bunch of people following her, one of which was carrying a cake. And they I'm looking at this going on, wondering what the heck is going on. And then I realized they were coming to my booth. Oh. A birthday cake. In all of her funny, ridiculous, fun loving self. And she was one of the other founders of IWS. You guys had some characters in those early years? Well, you know, there's characters all the time. Yeah. But anyway, I have never known anybody else to do anything quite like that. That was unforgettable birthday. That unforgettable birthday, that's for sure. So anyway, um, as far as I remember, um, Torgey Murdock. Katherine Torgerson Murdock of Pocatello was regional rep down there while I was in Idaho Falls. And we made many trips, uh, across the state, various meetings with IWS. And we got to be very good friends as well, and she and I had a lot of silly, but it was more private. Julie was right out there in front of everybody doing the belly dancing. I would not have been able to do that. But anyway, we had it was a smaller group then. It was easier to do things with the group. It's huge now. I mean, it has grown to be enormous big thing. I think there's probably more people in our region over here now than there used to be. Yes. The Organization is, I can't say that for sure, but I would suspect that's not far from the truth. And so it has really grown. And I think the fact that it has grown says that it's important. Yes, I. Agree, it's important to people to have IWS. I would not be, I would not be her without it. Well. No. Sincerely. It's very good. Um, okay. Well, I think one of the growth in your area is because of Les Scott, as your regional rep he has done such a great job in keeping art alive. He has, um. He's been amazing. And, uh, after I served for 12 or so years, um, Ginger Kramer did it for a similar length of time, and then Carlson did similar. Yeah. Yeah. And it just keeps growing. Mhm. And like I said, the fact that it's growing means it's important. Right. Yeah. It a volunteer group is a different kind of an organization because it runs on volunteers. And um, you know, unlike working for an organization where there are performance standards, you get just what a volunteer brings to the table. Yes, if you are willing to do it. That's very true. Absolutely true. And so it does. Like I said, it does have waves of ups and downs, but it just the core if it's wanted by people. Yes. Survives. Mhm. And someone will you know. But truthfully for those who do step up to the plate it's usually you gain like I said you gain, you learn a lot and you make friends and blah blah blah. It's it's not all giving. And so volunteer organizations and I have a lot of scouting Red cross and. Yeah. Volunteers are I think it's harder now because of of the volunteer workforce in America was a large part women. Now that so many women are occupied with an occupation, it leaves fewer people to do the volunteer work. Also a thing that is changing. Evolving. Volunteer. The whole volunteer scene? Yeah. And there's also a shift as well, where volunteerism used to be kind of the fabric of what we did or was a value. But I don't know that that's spoken of as much. Uh, the value of volunteering. I agree. I think when my mother was. A stay at home mother, and she volunteered for a lot of things, and her whole that whole a whole new feeling was to make your community better and volunteer as Volunteers for as much as you can do. I think that has changed. And, um, just because of lots of different reasons. But I don't know what. I don't know what the future of that is either. But and I think you ask about what I thought was good about. That is what has been for the volunteers who do. Yes, I would agree what you said about volunterring. You go in thinking you're going to contribute something and you end up gaining a lot. And for me, it's been the the relationships of getting to know others and be more invested in, um, IWS because everybody else is engaged in the drive to keep the organization going. Yes. Yeah. So I do think. I think you're absolutely right. Um. Let's see. I will include your, um, bio because there's so much about the many awards that you have gotten. What started you on that path towards, um, showing. Entering national shows? Uh, yeah. Um, well, it's actually pretty simple. I didn't get to study art in college. Um, and so,I feel like thousands of paintings is a good education, but when I was younger, I felt like I was I needed something to put down on a resume, or I just needed some kind of credentials. That's. Like Zippo. And so when I first started entering shows, it was actually my husband that encouraged me. You know, you should you should enter this. And I said, oh, you know me, I don't know. Well. It created a monster because once you start entering, you just keep entering. Anyway, I did, but that is the reason that I started, um, I just, I, I realized I can't even remember how I found out that if I, if I sent a slide, which is what we used to send. Mhm. Uh, if I send a slide for AWS or whatever, that I would get the catalog of the show. So I thought, well okay, it cost me $30 to enter and the catalog is worth that to me. Uh huh. And I thought, well I'll probably never get in, but I will try and I will get that catalogue and I'll see what did get in. And I'll learn some names and I'll just because that's just it was at first. Well, I got rejected many times for I never got accepted. And I just said, uh, well, I have the catalog and I have entered the show. In other words, I'm working. I'm, I'm working whether I get in or not, I'm working. And, um, I think it was Rudyard Kipling that said, treat those two imposters the same successes and failures. Treat them the same because it and I have adopted that and said, I'm working. I'm, I'm working. I don't believe in talent. You believe in work. Um. And, uh. And I'm a workhorse. I could tell. So I don't I don't mind working. I don't care if I'm what I'm doing in the studio. I can be doing calligraphy. I don't care what I'm doing, but I love being in my studio, and I love. I love the work. I don't like cutting mats. I don't like keeping records, but I do like a lot that goes on in the studio. So anyway, um, that's what why I actually started. And then I don't remember how many times I was rejected from AWS. That was the first one. I tried the first national one, but little did I know, it was like. But it was in New York, and I had lived in new Jersey for years, and I. Being on the East Coast then. Um, but, um, I just felt that I would just try. And the first one that I got, the very first time I was accepted in AWS, I was a shocked, but I was very shocked because it was not anywhere near my best. I had already entered my best in that year in other juried shows and I thought, well, I the only one I got left is this one. I'll send it. Mhm. It's the one that got in. Well that was a really big surprise to me. Mhm. But anyway that's kind of how I, how it got started. And then after a while I just started getting lots. I have far more rejection slips than I have accepted slips. But you know what happens if you rejected. You just say good I don't have to ship the darn thing. You know, it almost surprised me when you said you were rejected multiple times. Multiple times. Yeah. Knowing your work is like, oh, how could that happen? Well, it still happens that I mean, no, it's never a given to get into a show. Um, because I always say the only time I ever agree with the judge is when I'm the judge. Because I don't ever. You know, you don't. It's all very subjective. Yes. You have to be a bit of a duck and just let it roll off of you and say, well, try again next time. Right. It sounds like there are many, uh, internal strengths that you have that have helped you be a successful artist like your duck philosophy. I have a gift of Persistence. And I think that it behooves people to write down some of their intangible tools that they bring to the table. And persistence is intangible, but there are many, many that other people have. And I think it's that's one of the things when I teach that I like for people to think about intangible, because we all have the same tools, so we can buy the same brushes, we can buy the same those tools. But intangible tools are the ones that you have. Nobody else has. They're yours. And those frequently don't pay any attention to them. And yet they are what makes our art, our art. Right. Are where the magic is. And so they are very important. And persistence happens to be one. Yes. I'm Doggedly. I read about something that you had an intersting piec on goal setting on the internet. And, uh, how did you use goal setting to, um, in creating your success as an artist? You cut out the first part of that sentence. So the question was how I used Goal setting. Goal. Setting. Mhm. Okay. No, I, I truly, truly believe in goals. Little goals. Big goals. Like today I'm going to paint a painting with nothing but orange and blue. That's a goal. You know it can be a little goal but it's enough reason to paint it. Mhm. I remember when years and years ago I used to if I had a, you know inspirations of things that you really, really want to paint. That's that's, uh. And that's all well and good, but sometimes you have a little time to paint and you don't have that inspirational thing you want to paint. So why not say, I'm gonna paint something in orange and blue? I mean, it's a goal and that gets you and that gets the gets it going. Mhm. This is flowing. And pretty soon you're whatever. And orange and blue and it's been great. So goals are good but big goals are good also. And I think for people who want to enter shows which is not for everybody but people do that I can understand. Uh or it doesn't really make any difference what your goals are that you want to do with art, but I think if you recognize them and name them and claim them, you have a much better chance of accomplishing them. Right? Otherwise, It's kind of, you know, air in the sky. Right? And there's no accountability to yourself. Right? So I really do believe that for everything in life, yes, there's little goals and call it a goal. I have a goal to paint an orange and green day. Mhm. It seems very simple but it has a direction right. It gives you. I do and they have been very important. That's also probably one. Well is there, is there anything you would like to add to this interview that we haven't touched on? Well, um. I think that I would just say that art is more important than the world gives it, I think. And it doesn't have to be professional art, but having, you know, everything, your shirt that you're wearing is that beautiful design on it. Somebody did that, you know, somebody designed that and it goes nice with the painting behind you. But, um, I, I think. One of the more important things I think are art and belonging to it is because you're with people who do that and not everybody does. Um, and it gets cut from school a lot. Um, and yet I think it's really important to because when you are, when you're Drawing. Let's just take drawing. Just that one part of your drawing. You are seeing. And when you are seeing, you are appreciating. And you see things and change it. I feel sad sometimes with youth that don't, you know, they don't get that chance. And I don't think everybody has to be an artist. But I think having drawing in school, some drawing to teach people to see and, yes, and to see our planet through space, um, is a very valuable thing. And you're lucky to be. Yes, but it has to do with honoring life. How can you disrupt it? You know you can't be disruptive. Or even interested. Good thing. Yes I agree. Um, this sound is. I Oh, okay. That's good. I'm glad to hear that. Um, yeah, It is a way of honoring your life and what's around you, and to see it and try to reproduce it like a cave drawing. Those were just an artistic attempt to reflect what was important to them. It is a human. Uh, it's a human trait that I think we might be the only ones in the animal kingdom that try to do that? Yes. And it's. It can. It's primitive. Art is really interesting. People all over the world, throughout history have decorated things, have decorated themselves. Yeah. Their pottery decorated their walls. People are. Are interested in art. They know it or not. We'll just say that. So as artists, we get to be really interested in. Uh huh. It's a big connection you're making. It's very profound. Well, I don't know if it is or not, but it's just, uh. I actually went to Australia this last year, and one of the things that I really hoped to do was to buy a piece of Aboriginal Australian Aboriginal person and I. I needed a painting like I needed a hole in the head, but I really loved getting that from her. And it's it's very primitive and it's very Australian, you know dots, they tend to do that. And, um, I just find all of that stuff. Um. So anyway, it's a big subject. Yeah. There's a lot that goes with the songlines of the Australian art. lines.line: # Introduction and Interview Permissions

> Nancy Inaba introduces herself and the purpose of the interview, which is to document the history of the Idaho Watercolor Society as it approaches its 50th anniversary. She outlines the support from the Idaho Historical Society and the America 250 commemoration, emphasizing the project's mission to honor American ideals. Nancy also explains the structure of the interview, seeks Gloria's permission to record, and asks her to state and spell her name, establishing the formal start of the oral history session.

metadata.title: Introduction and Interview Permissions
metadata.synopsis: Nancy Inaba introduces herself and the purpose of the interview, which is to document the history of the Idaho Watercolor Society as it approaches its 50th anniversary. She outlines the support from the Idaho Historical Society and the America 250 commemoration, emphasizing the project's mission to honor American ideals. Nancy also explains the structure of the interview, seeks Gloria's permission to record, and asks her to state and spell her name, establishing the formal start of the oral history session.

lines.line: # Gloria's Early Life and Artistic Aspirations

> Gloria shares memories from her childhood, including her early dream of becoming a Disney artist and her perception of artists as being on a pedestal. She describes her upbringing in Kansas and Missouri, her family's artistic inclinations, and how her passion for art persisted throughout her life. Gloria also touches on her family life, her marriage, and how her identity as an artist grew stronger as her children became more independent.

metadata.title: Gloria's Early Life and Artistic Aspirations
metadata.synopsis: Gloria shares memories from her childhood, including her early dream of becoming a Disney artist and her perception of artists as being on a pedestal. She describes her upbringing in Kansas and Missouri, her family's artistic inclinations, and how her passion for art persisted throughout her life. Gloria also touches on her family life, her marriage, and how her identity as an artist grew stronger as her children became more independent.

lines.line: # Moving to Idaho and Discovering the Art Community

> Gloria recounts the journey that brought her to Idaho, which began with her husband's academic and professional pursuits. After living in New Jersey, the family moved to Idaho in 1970 for a job at the Idaho National Laboratory. Gloria describes her pleasant surprise at finding a vibrant art community in Idaho Falls, which played a significant role in her becoming a charter member of the Idaho Watercolor Society.

metadata.title: Moving to Idaho and Discovering the Art Community
metadata.synopsis: Gloria recounts the journey that brought her to Idaho, which began with her husband's academic and professional pursuits. After living in New Jersey, the family moved to Idaho in 1970 for a job at the Idaho National Laboratory. Gloria describes her pleasant surprise at finding a vibrant art community in Idaho Falls, which played a significant role in her becoming a charter member of the Idaho Watercolor Society.

lines.line: # Idaho Falls Art Community and Its Uniqueness

> The conversation highlights the unique and supportive nature of the Idaho Falls art community. Gloria discusses the history and influence of the Eagle Rock Art Guild, the growth of various art organizations, and the diversity of artistic groups available in the area. She emphasizes the strong community support for the arts, which distinguishes Idaho Falls from other places in the state.

metadata.title: Idaho Falls Art Community and Its Uniqueness
metadata.synopsis: The conversation highlights the unique and supportive nature of the Idaho Falls art community. Gloria discusses the history and influence of the Eagle Rock Art Guild, the growth of various art organizations, and the diversity of artistic groups available in the area. She emphasizes the strong community support for the arts, which distinguishes Idaho Falls from other places in the state.

lines.line: # Balancing Art and Family Life

> Gloria explains how she prioritized her children during their early years, participating in their activities and integrating art into scouting and other youth programs. She reflects on how her artistic pursuits expanded as her children grew older, eventually transitioning from a hobby to a central part of her identity. Gloria expresses no regrets about her choices, emphasizing the importance of focusing on family before fully dedicating herself to art.

metadata.title: Balancing Art and Family Life
metadata.synopsis: Gloria explains how she prioritized her children during their early years, participating in their activities and integrating art into scouting and other youth programs. She reflects on how her artistic pursuits expanded as her children grew older, eventually transitioning from a hobby to a central part of her identity. Gloria expresses no regrets about her choices, emphasizing the importance of focusing on family before fully dedicating herself to art.

lines.line: # Roles in Idaho Watercolor Society and Regional Representation

> Gloria describes her involvement as a regional representative for the Idaho Watercolor Society, following in the footsteps of her predecessor. She shares her experiences hosting nationally recognized watercolor artists for workshops across Idaho, the learning opportunities these events provided, and the mutual benefits of volunteering. Gloria also discusses how her role led to teaching opportunities and her growth as both an artist and educator.

metadata.title: Roles in Idaho Watercolor Society and Regional Representation
metadata.synopsis: Gloria describes her involvement as a regional representative for the Idaho Watercolor Society, following in the footsteps of her predecessor. She shares her experiences hosting nationally recognized watercolor artists for workshops across Idaho, the learning opportunities these events provided, and the mutual benefits of volunteering. Gloria also discusses how her role led to teaching opportunities and her growth as both an artist and educator.

lines.line: # Teaching, Workshops, and Philosophy on Creativity

> Gloria elaborates on her approach to teaching workshops, which go beyond technical skills to focus on creativity and self-discovery. She discusses her belief that everyone has an innate sense of design and creativity, and her goal is to help students recognize and develop these qualities. Gloria reflects on the importance of giving oneself permission to claim the title of 'artist,' sharing her own journey toward self-acceptance and the transformative impact it had on her perspective and work.

metadata.title: Teaching, Workshops, and Philosophy on Creativity
metadata.synopsis: Gloria elaborates on her approach to teaching workshops, which go beyond technical skills to focus on creativity and self-discovery. She discusses her belief that everyone has an innate sense of design and creativity, and her goal is to help students recognize and develop these qualities. Gloria reflects on the importance of giving oneself permission to claim the title of 'artist,' sharing her own journey toward self-acceptance and the transformative impact it had on her perspective and work.

lines.line: # Reflections on Idaho Watercolor Society's Successes and Challenges

> Gloria reflects on her years of active involvement with the Idaho Watercolor Society, noting the significance of bringing in national instructors and holding juried shows as key factors in the organization's vibrancy. She discusses the evolving landscape of art education with the rise of online resources and expresses uncertainty about the future of traditional workshops. Gloria also highlights the importance of friendships and community among artists, which provide support and understanding in a typically solitary profession.

metadata.title: Reflections on Idaho Watercolor Society's Successes and Challenges
metadata.synopsis: Gloria reflects on her years of active involvement with the Idaho Watercolor Society, noting the significance of bringing in national instructors and holding juried shows as key factors in the organization's vibrancy. She discusses the evolving landscape of art education with the rise of online resources and expresses uncertainty about the future of traditional workshops. Gloria also highlights the importance of friendships and community among artists, which provide support and understanding in a typically solitary profession.

lines.line: # Memorable Moments and Founders of the Idaho Watercolor Society

> Gloria shares memorable stories about the founders of the Idaho Watercolor Society, including Ruth Clark's initiative in starting the organization and Julie Rifle's playful personality, exemplified by a humorous birthday celebration. She recounts the camaraderie and unique characters that shaped the early years of the society, as well as the close friendships formed through shared experiences and travel for society events.

metadata.title: Memorable Moments and Founders of the Idaho Watercolor Society
metadata.synopsis: Gloria shares memorable stories about the founders of the Idaho Watercolor Society, including Ruth Clark's initiative in starting the organization and Julie Rifle's playful personality, exemplified by a humorous birthday celebration. She recounts the camaraderie and unique characters that shaped the early years of the society, as well as the close friendships formed through shared experiences and travel for society events.

lines.line: # Growth of the Organization and Volunteerism

> The discussion turns to the growth of the Idaho Watercolor Society and the crucial role of volunteers in sustaining the organization. Gloria acknowledges the contributions of successive regional representatives and notes the challenges facing volunteerism today, including societal changes and the decreasing availability of volunteers. She reflects on the personal rewards of volunteering and the evolving nature of community involvement.

metadata.title: Growth of the Organization and Volunteerism
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to the growth of the Idaho Watercolor Society and the crucial role of volunteers in sustaining the organization. Gloria acknowledges the contributions of successive regional representatives and notes the challenges facing volunteerism today, including societal changes and the decreasing availability of volunteers. She reflects on the personal rewards of volunteering and the evolving nature of community involvement.

lines.line: # Entering National Shows and Dealing with Rejection

> Gloria explains her motivation for entering national art shows, initially seeking credentials to compensate for not having formal art education. Encouraged by her husband, she began submitting her work, valuing the experience and the opportunity to learn from show catalogs even when rejected. Gloria emphasizes the importance of persistence, sharing her experiences with both rejection and acceptance, and her philosophy of treating success and failure with equanimity.

metadata.title: Entering National Shows and Dealing with Rejection
metadata.synopsis: Gloria explains her motivation for entering national art shows, initially seeking credentials to compensate for not having formal art education. Encouraged by her husband, she began submitting her work, valuing the experience and the opportunity to learn from show catalogs even when rejected. Gloria emphasizes the importance of persistence, sharing her experiences with both rejection and acceptance, and her philosophy of treating success and failure with equanimity.

lines.line: # Goal Setting and Artistic Success

> Gloria discusses the importance of goal setting in her artistic practice, from small daily objectives to larger ambitions like entering shows. She believes that clearly defined goals provide direction and motivation, increasing the likelihood of achievement. Gloria encourages artists to recognize and claim their goals, emphasizing that accountability and intentionality are key to artistic growth and fulfillment.

metadata.title: Goal Setting and Artistic Success
metadata.synopsis: Gloria discusses the importance of goal setting in her artistic practice, from small daily objectives to larger ambitions like entering shows. She believes that clearly defined goals provide direction and motivation, increasing the likelihood of achievement. Gloria encourages artists to recognize and claim their goals, emphasizing that accountability and intentionality are key to artistic growth and fulfillment.

lines.line: # The Importance of Art and Artistic Vision

> Gloria shares her views on the broader significance of art, stressing its value beyond professional achievement. She laments the reduction of art education in schools and highlights the role of drawing in teaching people to see and appreciate the world. Gloria believes that artistic expression is a fundamental human trait, connecting people across cultures and history, and that engaging with art is a way of honoring life and fostering deeper understanding.

metadata.title: The Importance of Art and Artistic Vision

She laments the reduction of art education in schools and highlights the role of drawing in teaching people to see and appreciate the world. Gloria believes that artistic expression is a fundamental human trait, connecting people across cultures and history, and that engaging with art is a way of honoring life and fostering deeper understanding.

lines.line: # Closing Remarks and Reflections

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude and acknowledgment of the interview's preservation in the Idaho Historical Society archives. Gloria and Nancy discuss the significance of documenting these stories for future generations and mention upcoming events and reunions. The conversation ends on a note of appreciation for the opportunity to reflect on a lifetime of artistic involvement and community.

children.text: # Introduction and Interview Permissions

> Nancy Inaba introduces herself and the purpose of the interview, which is to document the history of the Idaho Watercolor Society as it approaches its 50th anniversary. She outlines the support from the Idaho Historical Society and the America 250 commemoration, emphasizing the project's mission to honor American ideals. Nancy also explains the structure of the interview, seeks Gloria's permission to record, and asks her to state and spell her name, establishing the formal start of the oral history session.
# Gloria's Early Life and Artistic Aspirations

> Gloria shares memories from her childhood, including her early dream of becoming a Disney artist and her perception of artists as being on a pedestal. She describes her upbringing in Kansas and Missouri, her family's artistic inclinations, and how her passion for art persisted throughout her life. Gloria also touches on her family life, her marriage, and how her identity as an artist grew stronger as her children became more independent.
# Moving to Idaho and Discovering the Art Community

> Gloria recounts the journey that brought her to Idaho, which began with her husband's academic and professional pursuits. After living in New Jersey, the family moved to Idaho in 1970 for a job at the Idaho National Laboratory. > The conversation highlights the unique and supportive nature of the Idaho Falls art community. Gloria reflects on the importance of giving oneself permission to claim the title of 'artist,' sharing her own journey toward self-acceptance and the transformative impact it had on her perspective and work.
